role_hints.role: Research scientists (biology, chemistry, medical science core)
role_hints.oews_basis: 19-1042 Medical Scientists 6.1% of wage bill + 19-1021 Biochemists and Biophysicists 2.2% + other life-science research occupations in the unlisted tail (chemists, microbiologists, toxicologists), est. ~15-20% more (role_mix is the 5-digit 54171 proxy)
role_hints.note: Literature review, study design drafts, data analysis, regulatory writing and report/manuscript production are heavily AI-exposed; wet-lab experimentation, assay execution and in-vivo work are physical. Plausible within-role fraction 0.2-0.4.

role_hints.oews_basis: 19-4021 Biological Technicians 1.5% listed + a large unlisted tail of lab techs and research associates, est. ~8-12% more
role_hints.note: Hands-on bench work — cell culture, sample prep, assay running, animal care. Lab-automation-augmented but physically present; robotics, not LLMs, is the substitution channel.

role_hints.oews_basis: 11-9121 Natural Sciences Managers 7.3% + 11-9199 Managers All Other 3.1%
role_hints.note: Sponsor relationships, program direction and scientific judgment persist; internal reporting, resourcing and proposal assembly automate.

role_hints.oews_basis: 11-1021 General and Operations Managers 5.2% + 13-1082 Project Management Specialists 2.6% + 13-1199 Business Operations Specialists 1.6%
role_hints.note: Study coordination, contract administration and compliance paperwork automate heavily; sponsor-facing project leadership persists.

role_hints.role: Software developers and computational biology staff
role_hints.oews_basis: 15-1252 Software Developers 6.6% + data science occupations in the tail, est. ~2-3% more
role_hints.note: Bioinformatics pipelines, instrument software and data platforms; AI-augmented rather than eliminated.

role_hints.oews_basis: not in listed top-10; QA/RA occupations in the tail, est. ~5-8%
role_hints.note: GLP/GMP documentation, audit trails and submission assembly are heavily AI-exposed; accountable sign-off and inspection response persist.

role_hints.oews_basis: not in listed top-10; 54171 top-10 covers only ~38% of wage bill — admin, facilities/EHS, animal facility and misc. staff
role_hints.note: Admin and document work automates; vivarium, facilities and EHS staff are physical.

source_hints.source: NSF Business Enterprise Research and Development (BERD) Survey
source_hints.why: Official business R&D data — frames the funded-R&D vs fee-for-service composition of this code.

source_hints.source: BCG / Wellcome and similar published analyses of AI in drug discovery
source_hints.why: Documents how far AI has actually penetrated discovery workflows — grounds current_adoption_pct for research services.

source_hints.source: ISR Reports (Industry Standard Research) CRO market and outsourcing benchmarks
source_hints.why: Sponsor-side data on CRO selection, pricing models (FTE vs fee-for-service) and outsourcing penetration — core evidence for the capture question.

source_hints.source: Provident Healthcare Partners pharma services M&A coverage
source_hints.why: Deal activity and multiples in pharma services / preclinical CRO consolidation — for active_consolidators and exit_mult.

source_hints.source: Inotiv and other public preclinical-CRO filings and acquisition histories
source_hints.why: A documented small-lab acquisition spree (2021-2023) with disclosed prices — direct buy-multiple evidence at the small end; verify current pace.

source_hints.source: BIO (Biotechnology Innovation Organization) emerging-company and funding reports
source_hints.why: Sizing the venture-funded, pre-revenue population that inflates firm counts but is not acquirable on an EBITDA basis.

capture_questions: Contract research is priced as FTE-based agreements (billed per scientist-year), fee-for-service/unit pricing (per study, per assay), or milestone contracts — under FTE pricing, AI productivity gains directly cut what the sponsor buys; under unit pricing the operator keeps them until competitors reprice. What is the pricing-model mix for EBITDA-positive service firms in this code, and is sponsor procurement already pushing FTE-rate transparency?
Who owns the sponsor relationship — the firm (preferred-provider agreements, master service agreements with pharma) or individual scientific founders whose reputations carry the book?

What stops pharma/biotech sponsors from insourcing AI-accelerated discovery and analysis, outsourcing only the physical wet-lab and in-vivo work — and is outsourced discovery spend already shifting that way?

For the venture-biotech slice: is there any capture question at all, given these firms have no clients and no EBITDA — and does their presence in the code distort every industry statistic the run will find?
terminal_value_question: The mechanisms that hold paid demand are physical and regulatory: GLP animal studies, cell culture and assay execution cannot be done by AI, and FDA submissions require conducted studies, not predictions. The question that decides terminal value: as AI-first discovery compresses the analytical services CROs bill for (and possibly reduces the number of wet experiments needed per program), does the dominant sub-segment's revenue sit in the physical/regulated slice that survives, or the analytical slice AI replaces — i.e., is AI cutting this operator's delivery costs or shrinking the outsourced-research product it sells?
special_notes: HETEROGENEOUS R&D CODE: 541714 mixes (a) venture/grant-funded biotech ventures with no product revenue or EBITDA, (b) fee-for-service contract research and discovery labs (preclinical CROs, assay shops), and (c) hybrid platform companies. Identify the top-3 sub-segments, score the DOMINANT acquirable one, and set heterogeneous: true. Best hypothesis, flagged as hypothesis: fee-for-service contract research / preclinical services is the dominant EBITDA-generating, roll-up-relevant sub-segment; the venture-funded slice should be excluded from B.

Business-model trap on the dataset: labor_share is 0.9006 because many firms' 'receipts' understate what actually funds their wage bill (equity and grant funding are not receipts) — the near-1 labor share is a signature of the pre-revenue population, not of service-firm economics. Do not re-research the input (rule 6), but interpret V/economics for the service sub-segment, not the blended code, and flag in top_uncertain_inputs.

n_band caution: the 599-firm band estimate applies a generic 15.65% services margin to receipts — meaningless for pre-revenue ventures and rough for CROs; treat as an upper bound on acquirable cash-flowing targets.

Pricing-model deflation candidate: FTE-based research agreements are the industry's dominant large-sponsor structure and pass AI hour-savings to sponsors; fee-for-service/unit pricing is more capture-friendly — model pi_dist by mechanism mix.

Consolidation context (B/M only, rule 2): preclinical and specialty CRO roll-ups are well documented (Inotiv's acquisition run; PE platforms in bioanalytical labs); large-CRO mergers (ICON/PRA-scale) are a different market tier — do not import their multiples to small-lab buys.

Boundary confusion: clinical-stage CROs and central labs sit partly in 541380/6216xx/541714 depending on activity; diagnostics and manufacturing (CDMOs) are 325412/334516 — 'CRO market' reports span far more than this code; check definitions before quoting market sizes or multiples.
